coredata.dc:title: CDs@Cr2O3 catalytic degradation of Orange II based on non-radical pathway
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Lingli
Xiao, Qixing
Zhu, Lijun
Komarneni, Sridhar
Ma, Jianfeng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs)-modified Cr2O3 (CDs@Cr2O3) composites were synthesized by solvothermal process followed by calcination to improve their catalytic performance. The physical and chemical properties of the composites were determined by XRD, SEM, TEM, BET and XPS. With the aid of sodium persulfate, the activity of CDs@Cr2O3 to oxidize Orange II by non-radicals was studied under various conditions. The experimental results determined the optimal dosage of PDS to be 0.1 g and the degradation results showed that after 45 min, the degradation rate of Orange II reached to 98.4% with the CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system. The adsorption capacity of CDs@Cr2O3 for Orange II was closely related to pH, and this phenomenon is further explained by the isoelectric point. A series of experiments proved that non-free radicals played a crucial role in the oxidation process, in which singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide radical (O2 •−) and photo-generated holes (h+) were determined as the important reactive oxygen species (ROS). In addition, the present study found that increasing dissolved oxygen could promote the reaction and the catalyst maintained good degradation performance after five cycles suggesting its stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs-modified Cr2O3
Sodium persulfate
Non-radical activated
Singlet oxygen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance of the catalyst for degradation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( SBK2016021419 ) and ‘‘333 project” of Jiangsu Province . Innovation and entrepreneurship training program for college students in Jiangsu Province (No. 202110292062Y). Postgraduate Research Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX21_2874).

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (SBK2016021419) and ‘‘333 project” of Jiangsu Province. Innovation and entrepreneurship training program for college students in Jiangsu Province (No. 202110292062Y). Postgraduate Research Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX21_2874).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of experimental setup.
XRD patterns of CDs@Cr2O3 and pure Cr2O3.
SEM images of Cr2O3 (a) and CDs@Cr2O3 (b) and TEM images of Cr2O3 (c) and CDs@Cr2O3 (d–f).
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and derived pore-size distributions (inset) of CDs@Cr2O3 and Cr2O3.
XPS characterization: (a) Survey spectrum, (b) C 1, (c) O 1s and (d) Cr2p spectrum.
(a)Removal rate of dye under different conditions.(b)Pseudo-first-order kinetics fit for dye degradation in different systems.

Effects of PDS dosage on the degradation of Orange II in CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Effects of initial pH on Orange II in CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system.(b) Zeta potential of CDs@Cr2O3with different pH. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) The adsorption of MB at different pHs with CDs@Cr2O3.(b) Degradation of MB and TC solution in CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system.

Degradation of Orange II under different quenchers in the CDs@Cr2O3/PDS system. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Effects of (a) different quenchers and (b) different concentration of MeOH on the degradation of Orange II in the CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Effects of dissolved oxygen on the degradation of Orange II in CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The possible mechanism of Orange II degradation in CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Five cyclic degradation on Orange II dye in CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system.(b) XRD of the original CDs@Cr2O3 sample and used CDs@Cr2O3 sample. . (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Crystallite size, phase, structures, particle size, and BET analysis of catalysts.
Comparison of this work with the degradation activities of different catalysts.

head.ce:title: CDs@Cr2O3 catalytic degradation of Orange II based on non-radical pathway

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Safety Engineering, Changzhou University, Jiangsu, 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Safety Engineering, Changzhou University, Jiangsu, 213164, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ecosystem Science and Management and Materials Research Institute, 204 Energy and Environment Laboratory, The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, PA, 16802, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Ecosystem Science and Management and Materials Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Ecosystem Science and Management and Materials Research Institute, 204 Energy and Environment Laboratory, The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, PA, 16802, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs)-modified Cr2O3 (CDs@Cr2O3) composites were synthesized by solvothermal process followed by calcination to improve their catalytic performance. The physical and chemical properties of the composites were determined by XRD, SEM, TEM, BET and XPS. With the aid of sodium persulfate, the activity of CDs@Cr2O3 to oxidize Orange II by non-radicals was studied under various conditions. The experimental results determined the optimal dosage of PDS to be 0.1 g and the degradation results showed that after 45 min, the degradation rate of Orange II reached to 98.4% with the CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system. The adsorption capacity of CDs@Cr2O3 for Orange II was closely related to pH, and this phenomenon is further explained by the isoelectric point. A series of experiments proved that non-free radicals played a crucial role in the oxidation process, in which singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide radical (O2 •−) and photo-generated holes (h+) were determined as the important reactive oxygen species (ROS). In addition, the present study found that increasing dissolved oxygen could promote the reaction and the catalyst maintained good degradation performance after five cycles suggesting its stability.

ce:section.ce:para:
The rapid development of the economy has also brought about many environmental and water problems. The relationship between people and the environment is receiving much more attention because the restoration of the water environment has become imminent [1]. Persulfate (PS) -based advanced oxidation process (SR-AOPs) is regard as an important method in AOPs for its stronger oxidation capacity and wider pH range [2]. Extensive research has shown that nanomaterials such as sulfides [3], nitrides [4], metal oxides [5,6] and carbon materials [7] can be used to activate persulfate to degrade pollutants efficiently by free radical pathways and non-radical pathways. It is common knowledge that the radicals (•OH and SO4 •−) have high oxidation potentials, making them to play a significant role in catalytic oxidation processes [8]. Generally, peroxydisulfate (PDS, S2O8 2−) and peroxymonosulfate (PMS, HSO5 −) are the major precursors of •OH and SO4 •− radicals (Eq. (1) and (2)) [9].
(1) S2O8 2−→2SO4 •−
(2) HSO5 − →SO4 •− +•OH

Zhu et al. prepared CuS and applied it to degrade the Orange II. With K2S2O8 and visible light (Vis light) existing, CuS exhibited excellent reactivity and good recyclability, which is ascribed to the mass generation of •OH and SO4 •− [3]. In most cases, the process of activating persulfate is a combination of free radical and non-radical pathways [10–12], but the active site of the latter is often overlooked. Compared with the free radical pathways, the non-radical oxidation is hardly affected by anions in water (Cl−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, etc.) [13]. Besides, non-free radicals can efficiently oxidize salt-containing organic pollutants (such as dyes) [14]. More importantly, catalytic reactions based on non-radical pathways are safer than free radical pathways, as the latter may produce toxic by-products [15]. Therefore, more studies are needed to recognize the non-radical pathway mechanisms in the activation of PDS.

It is well known that singlet oxygen and electron transfer are the most common reactive oxygen species in non-radical oxidation processes [16]. Some catalysts have a carbonyl structure (CO, such as carbon materials) on the surface, which can activate persulfate to produce 1O2 with higher reactivity [14]. Carbonaceous materials, including nanodiamond [17], carbon nanotubes [18–20], biochar [21], graphene [22] and activated carbon [23] have been widely used in the field of photocatalysis. Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging carbon nanomaterials recently for their unique optical properties, good biocompatibility, low cost, and low toxicity [24]. However, single-component CDs exhibit poor photocatalytic activity due to their low photosensitive efficiency [25]. Moreover, CDs are difficult to recycle for their extremely small size and volume. Chen et al. utilized CDs to modify BiOCOOH while attempting to offset the UV limit of the photocatalyst and exhibited good stability [26]. Therefore, CDs have been considered as an ideal candidate for coupling with other catalysts owing to their excellent electron transfer capabilities and up-converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior, which will enhance the photocatalytic performance of pristine photocatalysts after coupling them together. The CDs/g-C3N4/ZnO nanocomposite photocatalyst reported by Guo et al. efficiently degraded tetracycline [27]. Zhang et al. successfully prepared CDs/Fe3O4@CS pomegranate-like composites by solvothermal method, in which singlet oxygen was proved to play a crucial role in ibuprofen degradation [28]. At present, there are many non-metal materials used to activate persulfate non-radical oxidation, but only a few metal oxides have been reported in non-radical activation, such as copper oxide (CuO) [29] and magnesium oxide (MgO) [30]. Cr2O3 is an important transition metal oxide with wide band gap energy of 3.4 eV leading to much attention because of its strong oxidation ability [31]. To the best of our knowledge, no studies exist about CDs doped Cr2O3 as a photocatalyst to activate persulfate to degrade dye wastewater.

The purpose of this work is to create a highly reactive catalyst and utilize the excellent electron transfer capabilities of CDs to improve the efficiency of activated PDS for the degradation of organics in wastewater. Therefore, CDs were successfully introduced on the surface of Cr2O3 by the solvothermal-calcination method. The non-radical oxidation was further confirmed by free radical scavenging experiments, and the degradation mechanism of singlet oxygen as the essential active oxygen species was identified. In addition, the effects of PDS dosage, initial pH value of pollutants, and dissolved oxygen on the degradation efficiency were explored and discussed in detail.

Glucose(C6H12O6),chromicnitratenonahydrate[Cr(NO3)3·9H2O],ethanol(C2H5OH), sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8), furfuryl alcohol (FFA) and triethanolamine (TEOA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai China). Orange II was provided by Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). P-benzoquinone (BQ), and methanol (MeOH) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Additionally, all the chemicals were used without any other purification.

CDs@Cr2O3was prepared by solvothermal-calcination two-step method. First, 1.6 g of glucose was dispersed in the mixed solution of 40 mL H2O and 60 mL ethanol [32]. 6.4 g Cr(NO3)3·9H2O was dispersed and mixed in the mixture, stirred for 2 h and then allowed to stand still for 12 h. The above suspension was transferred to an autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene, and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After hydrothermal carbonization, it was collected by centrifugation (3500 rpm, 10 min), and washed with distilled water and ethanol for 3 times. The obtain product was dried in a vacuum dryer overnight, and then calcined at 600 °C in N2 atmosphere for 2 h to obtain highly reactive chromium oxide [33,34]. The protection of nitrogen reduces the loss of carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs [35]. The final product is ground into powder and recorded as CDs@Cr2O3 for further use. The preparation process of Cr2O3was the same as the above steps, except adding glucose.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) equipped with Cu Kα (λ = 1.54056 Å) radiation of 0.154 nm wavelength was used in this research. The JSM-6360LA scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, Japan) was intended for detecting the surface morphology of the catalyst. The JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope (Japan)was used to acquire the transmission electronmicroscopy (TEM) images of CDs@Cr2O3.The chemical states of C, O and Cr in CDs@Cr2O3 were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI-Quantera SXM, Perkin-Elmer Co, USA). Zeta potentiometer (ZetasizerNano ZEN3600) was used to detect the isoelectric point of CDs@Cr2O3. The surface area was obtained by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method with an adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics, ASAP 2010C, USA).

All the degradation experiments were conducted at room temperature and Orange II was selected as the target pollutant. The catalytic activity of the sample was evaluated through three different catalytic degradation processes, namely: photocatalysis, chemical catalysis, and photochemical catalysis. Photochemical catalysis is a process in which photocatalysis and chemical catalysis work together. The light used in this experiment is 120 W light emitting diode (LED) incandescent lamp and the simplified diagram of the experimental setup is presented in Fig. 1 .

Orange II dye was degraded by adding 50 mg CDs@Cr2O3 sample to 100 mL of the 50 mg/L Orange II solution, with stirring continuously at 500 revolutions/min to mix well. The suspension reacted in the dark for 15min to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Corresponding to the three degradation processes mentioned above, the reaction was performed under different conditions. The photocatalytic process was carried out under visible light, while the chemical catalytic process by adding 100 mg Na2S2O8 to the reaction solution was conducted in the dark. The photochemical catalytic system worked in the presence of both PDS and Vis light. To test the remaining concentration of the Orange II, 2 mL suspension was extracted every 5 min and filtered by a 0.22 μm Millipore filter. Then, the resulting clear liquor was analyzed for absorbance at the Orange II maximum absorption wavelength of 484 nm with a UV–vis spectrophotomete.

A series of recovery tests were conducted so as to prove the stability and recyclability of CDs@Cr2O3. After the catalytic degradation experiment, the sample was collected and washed for 3 times and then dried at 80 °C. The collected material was used for the next reuse experiment.

The XRD patterns were obtained in Fig. 2 to investigate the crystallinity and phase purity of CDs@Cr2O3 and pure Cr2O3. The crystallite size, phase, and structure of the catalysts are shown in Table 1 . The diffraction peaks at 2θ degrees of 24.49°, 33.59°, 36.19°, 41.47°, 50.21°, 54.85°, 63.44° and 65.10° correspond to (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (214) and (300) planes of hexagonal eskolaite phase (JCPDS card No. 38–1479) [36]. The crystallite size calculated by Scherrer's formula [37] of the single Cr2O3 was approximately 20.6 nm and the CDs@Cr2O3 was about 22.2 nm. Compared with pure Cr2O3, the diffraction peaks of CDs@Cr2O3 appeared to be narrower but their intensity increased to a certain extent, which indicated a smaller size but better crystallinity. Additionally, no typical characteristic peak of CDs was observed due to their small quantity.

SEM images were intended for detecting the surface morphology of the catalysts and particle size of Cr2O3 and CDs is shown in Table 1. The micromorphology of pure Cr2O3 and CDs@Cr2O is displayed in Fig. 3 a and b. It can be seen that the single Cr2O3 presents flake like morphology (Fig. 3a), whose particle size is about 0.8–2.5 μm. After loading carbon dots, the shape of Cr2O3 did not change significantly, and it could be clearly observed in Fig. 3b that the carbon dots were tightly loaded on the Cr2O3. In order to gain further insights into the detailed morphological structure of materials, TEM images of Cr2O3 and CDs@Cr2O are provided in Fig. 3c–f. As shown in the micrograph, the white part on Cr2O3 is the pore structure (see the red outline in Fig. 3a) and the Cr2O3 has smooth edges. In Fig. 3d, the carbon dot particles are mostly spherical. Multiple CDs were aggregated and uniformly dispersed on Cr2O3 although agglomeration appeared occasionally, indicating that the CDs have been successfully compounded with Cr2O3. As presented in Fig. 3e and f, the carbon dots have a particle size of about 10–20 nm, and they are randomly aggregated to irregular shapes.

Specific surface area is also a key factor in catalytic activity. The specific surface areas of CDs@Cr2O3 and pure Cr2O3 catalysts were measured by BET and the results are displayed in Table 1. It can be obviously seen that CDs@Cr2O3 has a higher specific surface area (130.39 m2/g) than that of pure Cr2O3(54.30 m2/g) because of the increased surface area of pores between stacked carbon dots as particles. The adsorption-desorption isotherms of N2 and the corresponding pore size distribution curves on CDs@Cr2O3 and pure Cr2O3 are presented in Fig. 4 . The isotherms of both samples are classified as type IV suggesting that the material has a mesoporous structure [38]. It exhibited a H3 type hysteresis loop for CDs@Cr2O3, which indicates the presence of plate-like particles or slit-shaped pores in both samples. This is consistent with the morphology of SEM.

XPS study was done to reveal the elemental composition and valence states of the catalysts. The presence of C, O and Cr elements on the full-scan spectrum has is shown in Fig. 5 a, which confirms that the carbon dots were successfully attached to Cr2O3. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 5b)elucidates three peaks at 284.6eV, 285.8 eV and 288.4eV,representing the C–C bond, C–O–C, O–CO species respectively [39]. The O 1s is composed of three peaks at 530.2, 531.7 and 533.0 eV, which correspond to lattice oxygen (MeO) in Cr2O3, surface-adsorbed O and carbon–oxygen bond (C–O),respectively (Fig. 5c). Two peaks of the Cr2p spectrum at 576.8 and 586.0eV are associated with Cr3+ (Fig. 5d) and these two peaks belong to Cr 2p3/2 and Cr 2p1/2 respectively [40], which proved the presence of Cr2O3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the catalyst for degradation
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the feasibility of the CDs@Cr2O3 to promote the removal rate of organic pollutants, the removal efficiency of dye was studied as the target pollutant. A series of control experiments revealed that Orange II remained almost stable in the absence of the catalyst. According to Fig. 6 a, the blank test showed that the degradation activity of visible light against Orange II is negligible. PDS/Vis system also didn't show the ability to degrade pollutants, and the difference with no Vis light can be ignored. However, the removal rate of the pollutant by the CDs@Cr2O3 catalyst varied under different conditions.

The degradation experiment lasted for 45 min, and the suspension reached the equilibrium state of adsorption-desorption after 15 min. It is shown in Fig. 6a that CDs@Cr2O3 adsorbed 10% of the dye approximately, which is attributed to the modification of Cr2O3 by CDs to increase the specific surface area of the composite. In CDs/PDS/Vis system, only a small part of Orange II was degraded, suggesting that CDs themselves cannot be used as catalysts to directly activate PDS to produce active substances that are beneficial to degradation [25]. Because of the high activity of Cr3+/Cr4+, Cr2O3 could react with PDS to generate 1O2 (as shown in Eqs. (3) and (4)) [40], but the active sites of unmodified Cr2O3 are insufficient, so pure Cr2O3 is difficult to degrade pollutants efficiently and quickly.
(3) Cr3++S2O8 2−→Cr4++SO4 •−+SO4 2−
(4) O2 −+Cr4+ → 1O2+Cr3+

In contrast, the developed CDs@Cr2O3 nanocomposite exhibited an enhanced catalytic performance towards the degradation of Orange II. The chemical catalytic effect of CDs@Cr2O3 was evaluated by adding Na2S2O4 after the adsorption process to degrade Orange II in the dark. After the reaction, the removal rate of the dye by this system reached 88.7%, second only to the photochemical catalytic system (Fig. 6a). This can be attributed to the CDs@Cr2O3, which can be excited by Vis light to generate great number of hole-electron pairs. The O2 adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst acts as an acceptor for electrons (e−), and then it is converted into O2 •−, which may be the active species (Eq. (5)) [41].
(5) O2+e−→ O2 •−

Under the combined work of visible light and persulfate, the degradation rate of CDs@Cr2O3 to the dye reached 98.4% within 45 min. Without adding PDS and other conditions unchanged, about 27% of the organic dye molecules were degraded. Thus, it can be seen that persulfate is a vital factor affecting the catalytic reaction. The effect of adding different amounts of PDS on the reaction process is discussed in Fig. 7 . With the dosage of PDS increasing from 0.02 to 0.1 g, the degradation rate of Orange II had been dramatically improved. This phenomenon may be credited to the more reactive species such as 1O2 and O2 •− produced by PDS, which are beneficial to the degradation reaction.

The degradation of Orange II is approximately simulated by the one-dimensional kinetic model by the formula below:
-lnC/C
0
= kt

k is the kinetic constant [3]. Descending order of the kinetic constants of each system (Fig. 6b) from top to bottom is 0.05487, 0.0261, 0.00276, 0.00504 and 0.00143, respectively. Obviously, the rate constant of the CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system is much higher than others, indicating that this system is more efficient in degrading Orange II.

In addition, similar studies are compared in Table 2 to evaluate the degradation activity of the synthesized CDs@Cr2O3. Overall, the catalysts modified by CDs can degrade various organic pollutants rapidly and efficiently. At the same time, compared with other materials, CDs@Cr2O3 has faster degradation rate and higher degradation efficiency with lower catalyst dose.

As is known to all that the initial pH value is one of the vital factors affecting the degradation reaction [45]. Fig. 8 a shows the effects of different initial pH on the performance of CDs@Cr2O3 in the photochemical catalytic degradation of dyes by adding 0.1 mol/L NaOH and HCl. When pH value is in the range of 3–9, the CDs@Cr2O3 shows good catalytic properties, indicating that the material has a good adapt ability to pH. However, when the pH of the solution reached to 12, the reaction was significantly inhibited, removing only about 8.3% of the contaminants, probably because the excess base inhibited PDS adsorption on the surface of CDs@Cr2O3. Interestingly, the adsorption capacity of the catalyst far exceeds the neutral and alkaline conditions. The point of zero charge (PZC) value represents the zero charge of the catalyst, which is a necessary research object in the study of dye adsorption. In fact, when the (pHPZC) catalyst < pH(solution), the cationic dye molecule will be adsorbed, but when the (pHPZC) catalyst > pH(solution), the anionic dye molecule will be adsorbed [46]. Since the value of pHPZC for CDs@Cr2O3 = 5.3(Fig. 8b), the number of positive charges on the catalyst surface will increase when the solution pH is below 5.3, therefore, more Orange II dyes can be adsorbed in this case. Fig. 9 a shows the adsorption effects of CDs@Cr2O3 on the cationic dye (MB). Unlike the adsorption of Orange II, the adsorption capacity of MB is much greater when pH of the solution is equal to12 than when the pH of the solution is equal to 3. This result confirms the role of PZC and pH on dye adsorption. In addition, to study the practical adaptability of catalysts, MB and tetracycline (TC) were selected as model pollutants. As shown in Fig. 9b, the CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system can effectively degrade MB, and the whole reaction process was complete within 35 min. Nearly 82.0% of TC was removed within 55 min, indicating that the CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system had a strong ability to degrade antibiotic-containing wastewater.

In general, SO4 •− and •OH are the primary active substances in the persulfate advanced oxidation [47–50]. In recent years, many researchers have found that 1O2 and O2 •− can also effectively and rapidly degrade organic pollutants [4]. In this study, to explore the degradation mechanism of CDs@Cr2O3 in catalytic process, different quenchers were added to the chemical catalytic system and photochemical catalytic system as follows: methanol (MeOH) is an inhibitor of the SO4 •− and •OH, and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) is used as a scavenger of superoxide radicals. TEOA is a quencher of h+, and FFA is commonly used to inhibit 1O2 [45].

As can be seen in Fig. 11a, the inhibitory effect of methanol was the weakest in photochemical catalysis, with the degradation rate only decreasing by about 13.4%, which is much lower than that of other quenchers, suggesting that small amounts of free radicals were produced or consumed in the system. Subsequent experiments were carried out (Fig. 11b) by increasing methanol from 5 ml to 20 ml. However, no significant improvement in inhibition occurred, further confirming that the free radicals are not the predominant reactive matters in this system. FFA showed the most significant inhibitory effect on degradation, which means that non-radical oxidation is the main pathway of the catalytic reaction. When p-BQ and TEOA were added to the system, the removal rate of Orange II decreased from 98.4% to 30.4% and 43.3%, respectively. Hence, it can be deduced that 1O2, h+ and O2 •− are the main active oxygen species to degrade pollutants in the present photochemical catalytic system. Meanwhile, in the chemical catalytic system, FFA had the strongest inhibitory effect as well, followed by p-BQ, as shown in Fig. 10
. When the quenchers were added to the system, the reaction was inhibited to varying degrees. The degradation rate decreased to 19.7% and 48.0% under the influence of FFA and p-BQ, respectively. However, TEOA showed little inhibition of the degradation reaction. This means that 1O2 was the main active substances in this system. As described in Eq. (6), persistent free radicals (PFRs) on the surface of CDs@Cr2O3 lead to the production of O2 •− [40,51], which can participate in degradation reactions.
(6) CDs@Cr2O3 PFRS + O2→O2 •−+ CDs@Cr2O3 PFRS +

According to previous studies, dissolved oxygen can act as a precursor to generate ROS [52], such as •OH and O2 •−, which are beneficial to the catalytic reaction. Therefore, experiments were conducted under the condition of O2 continuous aeration to test the influence of dissolved oxygen in CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system. The results depicted in Fig. 12 revealed that high concentration of the oxygen promoted the removal of Orange II.

In view of the above researches, further explanation of the possible mechanism of the catalytic systems is proposed here and presented in Fig. 13 . When Cr2O3 was irradiated by Vis light, electrons and holes were produced on the catalyst surface. And the electrons on Cr2O3 tended to transfer to CDs, causing the photo-generated electrons and electron-hole pairs to be effectively separated (Eq. (7)). The electrons would constantly accumulate on the CDs and then combine with dissolved oxygen to form superoxide radicals [53] (Eq. (5)). The oxygen dissolved in the water drives the generation of O2 •− and promotes the reaction. Meanwhile, the available holes oxidized H2O to yield •OH [37,54], which then could participate in photochemical catalytic reaction (Eq. (8)). Consequently, The CDs played an important role because the electron-hole pairs would rapidly recombine without the CDs. Then, singlet oxygen(1O2) could be produced by the reaction of O2 •− with •OH (Eq. (9)). Besides, O2 •−would also react with h+ to form1O2(Eq. (10)) [55]. Moreover, Cr3+ in the catalyst could activate PDS to produce SO4 •− and Cr4+ (Eq. (3)), which would accept e− to form 1O2 and Cr3+ as given in Eq. (4). All the reactive species generated were ultimately used to degrade the contaminants into CO2 and H2O (Eq. (11)).
(7) Catalyst + hv →e−+ h+
(5a) O2+e−→O2 •−
(8) h++H2O→•OH+H+
(9) O2 •−+•OH→1O2+OH−
(10) h++O2 •−→1O2
(3a) Cr3++S2O8 2− → Cr4++SO4 •−+SO4 2−
(4a) O2 •−+Cr4+→1O2+Cr3+
(11)
1O2/ O2 •−/ h++Orange II→CO2+H2O

The cycling experiments were performed in the same condition to that of the fresh catalyst with the CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system for five cycles. As shown in Fig. 14 a, CDs@Cr2O3 still maintained high degradation rate of 97.4% after five cycles. Compared with some previous researchers, who performed only three cycles, we performed five cycles. Wang et al. reported 5.6% loss of ibuprofen degradation removal of five repetitive catalyst usage comparable to the fresh one [28]. The degradation rate exhibited by Zhang et al. was reduced to 81.3% after 60 min after the fifth repeat [42]. The X-ray diffraction patterns of the fresh and used CDs@Cr2O3 are compared in Fig. 14b. It is obvious that no other impurity diffraction peaks were detected, implying that the crystalline phase of the material remains unchanged. The above results suggest that the CDs@Cr2O3 catalyst has superior recyclability and catalytic activity for potential application in wastewater treatment.

In this work, the CDs@Cr2O3 composite was developed through a simple preparation method and a pathway to degrade the Orange II through non-radical means was proposed. Quenching experiments confirmed that singlet oxygen, pores and superoxide radicals were the major active substances during the reaction. The modification of carbon dots increased the electron transfer rate in solution and reduced the recombination of electron-hole pairs, resulting in more electrons and holes participating in the degradation reaction and accelerating the generation of reactive oxygen species. Meanwhile, increasing the concentration of dissolved oxygen can also effectively improve the degradation efficiency of the catalyst. The CDs@Cr2O3/PDS/Vis system exhibited excellent catalytic performance, wide adaptability to pH and different types of pollutants and good reusability. This study not only provides a feasible method for degrading dyes, but also provides more options for the application of non-radical oxidation in AOPs.

Lingli Ji: Writing – original draft. Lijun Zhu: Writing – review & editing. Sridhar Komarneni: Writing – review & editing. Jianfeng Ma: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (SBK2016021419) and ‘‘333 project” of Jiangsu Province. Innovation and entrepreneurship training program for college students in Jiangsu Province (No. 202110292062Y). Postgraduate Research Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX21_2874).
